summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Application for interim parenting orders made at the conclusion of the final hearing – Where the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer seek to cease all time and communication between the father and the children pending the making of final orders – Order made providing for the older child to spend time with the father in accordance with the child’s wishes – Order made reducing the time spent between the father and the younger child from a week about arrangement to three nights per fortnight.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

BACKGROUND

These are parenting proceedings concerning the children X born in 2013 and Y born in 2015 (collectively “the children”). X is presently living with the mother and does not currently spend time with the father. Y continues to live on a week about basis, in the care of each of his parents. The shared care arrangement has been in place (though in a different composition) since the parties separated on 25 February 2020. It is in this context, and for the reasons below, that these reasons primarily concern the interim care arrangements for Y.
Each of the children has additional needs and vulnerabilities. Y has been assessed as having:
(a) Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder; inattentive subtype (“ADHD”);

(b) Generalised social anxiety disorder with panic attacks;

(c) Emotional dysregulation; and

(d) Certain challenges in social settings and relating to school.

In further interim orders made by consent on 16 October 2024, the parties reiterated the week about care arrangement for Y (among other things).
The father, who is self-represented, indicated at the outset of the hearing that he had revised his orders sought with respect to arrangements concerning the child X. The father indicated that he no longer sought orders for X to spend defined time in his care but would instead support orders that X spend time with the father in accordance with his views. The father prepared a revised minute of orders sought which was disseminated the following day and which reflected that change.
The mother’s final orders sought were identified in her Amended Initiating Application filed on 26 May 2025. In that document, the mother proposed that she would have sole decision making responsibility for long term decisions for both children (but would advise the father after a decision was made), that Y’s time with his father would be reduced to a weekend (Friday to Monday) every second week, and for time during school holidays, among other things.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) filed and served an outline of case document on the afternoon of Sunday 8 March 2026 in which the ICL reserved her final position until the conclusion of the evidence. In that document, the ICL identified a number of risks for Y, including the risk of psychological harm arising from aspects of his father’s engagement with him (against a background of alleged family violence by the father to the mother and the children’s exposure to this). The ICL indicated that she did not support the continuation of the week about arrangement for Y and did not, at that stage, have a concluded view as to the arrangement which would be in Y’s best interests. She identified, however, that if an unacceptable risk of psychological harm exists for Y in spending time with the father:
... this means that the Court needs to grapple with whether it is in the best interests of [Y] to have his time with the father limited to three or four nights a fortnight, or to supervised time, ID time or no time.
The ICL also met with the children on 4 March 2026, and the outline of case document contained a statement of what each of the children had said to the ICL in that meeting, about their living arrangements and related matters. Of Y, the ICL reported:
(a) Y was genuinely concerned about his future live with arrangements.

(b) He finds the equal time hard to manage and has some sense of unfairness that his brother has been able to “vote with his feet” but he cannot. His views have been consistent.

(c) He described his father as having the power to “whip” him, threatening “I ought to hit you right now”. He does not feel connected or aligned to his stepmother and stepsiblings. He expressed a view that the father can put pressure on him to express what he wants to hear. This psychological coercion (“psychological torture”) appears to be really hard for Y to manage.

The final hearing proceeded over five days. On the final day, the ICL circulated a minute of orders in which she sought orders to the effect that Y live with his mother and spend no time with his father for a period of six months after the making of final orders. Thereafter, the ICL proposed that Y spend time with his father, in accordance with his wishes.
At the conclusion of the evidence on the fifth day, the mother’s counsel submitted that:
(a) The mother sought to revise her final parenting orders sought, such that instead of the relief set out in her Amended Initiating Application, she joined in seeking the relief sought by the ICL; and

(b) Further, the mother sought interim orders, to apply in the period while judgment was to be reserved, which would result in Y’s time with the father being suspended immediately and Y not spending time with, or communicating with, the father in that period (this was expressed as applying to both children, though at the time of the hearing, X was not spending time with the father). The mother also sought a series of restraints upon the father including that he not attend the mother’s home or her place of work, that he not approach the mother and that he not attend the children’s school and extra-curricular activities.

Having been satisfied that the father, who is self-represented in these proceedings, had been given notice of the interim orders sought, submissions commenced on day five of the hearing but were not concluded that afternoon. It was necessary to allocate additional time on subsequent days to enable the conclusion of submissions.
In circumstances where it was contended on behalf of the mother that there was an unacceptable risk of psychological harm to Y in spending time with the father, but where submissions had not concluded, I made an order which had the effect of ensuring that Y remained in the mother’s care until the hearing could be completed and the mother’s application for interim orders was determined.
These reasons address the interim relief sought by the mother and the ICL. They are delivered in short form pursuant to s 69ZL of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). In that context, I note that the court is not required to mention every fact or argument relied on as relevant to an issue in the proceeding. In Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; (2003) 200 ALR 447 at [62], Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ said:

...A judge’s reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge’s failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party’s case.

In circumstances where X is not presently spending time with the father and where the father seeks final orders that X spend time with him in accordance with the child’s wishes, these reasons address the arrangements to be put into place for Y pending delivery of my reasons on a final basis. I do not consider that, on an interim basis, it is necessary to make the order sought by the mother which would have the effect of ensuring that X spends no time with the father in circumstances where it is unchallenged that X is currently not spending time with the father. I instead consider it appropriate to make an order which enables X to spend time with the father, as X wishes. That had been the position for X for some time before the final hearing. Evidence of X’s experiences leading to the current arrangements in place for his care featured significantly at the final hearing and form part of the evidentiary matrix which informs the final orders to be made for these children. For the purposes of these reasons, however, the primary focus remains upon Y and his experiences and vulnerabilities.

THE MOTHER’S CASE

The mother’s interim application was premised on the submission that Y spending time with his father placed Y at an unacceptable risk of psychological harm, in part arising from the asserted unacknowledged (and therefore untreated) anger issues of the father. It was submitted that there was a risk to Y that his father may become angry and dysregulated and that he may then engage in retaliatory conduct (including if things do not happen in accordance with his views or preferences) to the child.
It was also submitted that particular events which had been the subject of close attention during the hearing would lead the court to find that the father was obstructive and oppositional to particular arrangements which were in the best interests of the children, when they did not accord with his views. It was submitted that the father did not have the capacity to respond to the needs of the children from an empathetic perspective. That asserted lack of empathy and insight into the needs of the children, coupled with the risk of the father engaging in an angry and dysregulated way when faced with opposition to his preferences, it was submitted, would place Y at an unacceptable risk of psychological harm in the father’s care.
Explicit in the mother’s interim application was the contention that the risk of psychological harm to Y in spending time with his father could not be mitigated to make it acceptable. The mother’s counsel contended that “business as usual” did not serve Y. As a consequence, the immediate suspension of Y’s time with his father was proposed. This outcome was supported by the ICL and opposed by the father.
A number of events the subject of particular attention at the final hearing were relied upon by the mother to support her interim application and her contentions about risk to Y when in the father’s care. In circumstances where the final hearing has occurred and concluded, the below reflects my acceptance of certain of the evidence and findings I make about some matters which were in dispute between the parties, so far as they are relevant to my determination of the care arrangements for Y pending the making of final orders.

The children’s psychologist

The mother contends that the father has shown a lack of insight into the needs of the children and has been incapable of prioritising their needs over his preferences, to their substantial detriment. While both children had engaged with a psychologist (“[Ms D]”) (since March 2022 for X and August 2022 for Y) and both appeared to feel supported by that engagement, the father resolved that she should not continue to provide care to the children (because of his suspicions about the purpose and benefit of the engagement). The mother did not agree to change treaters.
The father persisted in his view that Ms D was not suitable. He took steps to terminate Ms D’s engagement in January 2024, communicating to her directly that he had withdrawn his consent to her involvement with the children.
At the same time, both children were experiencing vulnerabilities, including, for X, deterioration of his mental health and increased suicidal ideation. X was resistant to spending time with the father. The evidence makes clear that each of the children liked Ms D and wanted to continue to work with her. So concerned was Ms D for the psychological safety of X in particular, that she wrote to the parties on 29 January 2024 urging against the abrupt termination of the children’s engagement with her.
The family therapist, Ms E, also urged the father (and communicated to both parents) to reinstate Ms D to provide the children the benefit of her professional support. At the same time, the father wrote to Ms E, expressing his view that the engagement with Ms D was “corrupt” and that he remained deeply sceptical of X’s reported suicidal ideation.
The father would not yield, and the mother was required to apply to the court to secure the continuation of the engagement with Ms D. On 5 March 2024, the father consented to orders providing for the children to continue to attend upon Ms D until another psychologist, recommended by the family therapist and agreed between the parties, could be engaged. I find that the father’s late consent to the reinstatement of Ms D was not suggestive of acquired insight into the children’s needs and the benefits they would derive from continuing with Ms D, given he advised the family therapist that it was “the optics” which compelled his reluctant compromise.
The father’s lack of insight was made clear when, despite the orders providing for the resumption of engagement with Ms D, the father obstructed those arrangements in April 2024 and communicated directly to Ms D that he did not consent to her seeing the children. Ms D wrote to the children’s school expressing her concerns for the mental health of both children and urging the reinstatement of mental health care for them (being particularly concerned for X’s well-being at that time).
The father made and lodged a highly critical public review of Ms D’s services and made a complaint to her professional supervisory body. The father falsely accused Ms D of engaging with the children contrary to court orders. Consequently, and despite the consent orders made on 5 March 2024, Ms D’s engagement with the children ceased, which I am satisfied, was the result of the father’s interference and obstruction.
Ms D prepared a report dated 26 July 2024 in which she provided information about her previous engagement with the children. Relevantly, Ms D opined of Y that consideration should be given to the level of unsupervised time he had with the father (which at that time was a shared care arrangement). She noted that Y expressed willingness and interest to spend time with the father, but she described him as being “clearly fearful” of him. She reported that Y was unsure about “withdrawing from the current contact arrangement because he can see the distress it has caused his brother and how angry it has made his father”.

Y’s health needs and medication

The mother also identifies the challenges she experienced in securing the father’s support relating to Y’s ADHD diagnosis and agreeing an appropriate treatment regime, as a further instance of his lack of insight into the needs of the children. While the father maintained in his oral evidence that he accepted the diagnosis from 2022, he was opposed to the child being medicated.
In a report from paediatrician Dr F, she recounts a meeting she had in March 2024 with the mother and Y and then separately, the father and his wife. In that document, Dr F recounts that the father had advised her that the children’s psychologist had said that the children did not require further sessions. I am satisfied that the father knew when he made that statement to Dr F that such was not truthful.
In circumstances where Y was experiencing challenges engaging at school, Y told Dr F that he wanted to try medication as his “brain is full of many things” and he was easily distracted.
By May 2024, Dr F had written to the parties recommending the commencement of a medication trial for Y. The father still opposed the commencement of medication for Y.
In June 2024, X was also referred to Dr F, in circumstances where he was experiencing school difficulties (bullying), performance anxiety and panic attacks and had low mood and self-esteem challenges.
On 22 July 2024, the father emailed Dr F objecting to her meeting with X without his knowledge and the opportunity to attend the appointments.
Subsequently, the father posted an online public review of Dr F that was highly critical of her services and expertise.
The mother applied to the court seeking orders relating to medication for Y and about management of the children’s engagement with Dr F. On 16 October 2024, an order was made by consent providing, among other things, for the commencement of medication for Y and for the mother to manage the children’s engagement with Dr F (and providing a scheme by which the father would provide information to Dr F with observations of the children and any matters he wished to raise with Dr F). Y’s week about living arrangement with each of the parents was again provided for in those orders.
The father remained concerned about the impacts of the drug regime upon Y and about side effects which he observed Y to be experiencing when the child was with him. The mother did not make the same observations.
The evidence makes clear, and I find, that in October 2025, the father determined to cease Y’s medication when Y was in his care, without the mother’s input or knowledge and without the advice and guidance of the paediatrician.
The father’s actions in doing so, caused Y to be placed in the middle of this dispute because he knew his father’s views and plans, when his mother and the school were not advised. The Single Expert agreed that this would have created psychological pressure for the child.

The family therapist

Ms E was appointed by the parties to engage in family therapy. Her first period of engagement occurred between 1 February and 11 March 2024.
On 7 March 2024, Ms E facilitated a session with the family and gave feedback to the parents about matters explored with the children and things they said they were experiencing.
Ms E reported of Y, in her notes of that session, that he was too scared to tell his father about what he was feeling, and he described his feelings when the father yelled and the tone he used. Y reported that his father “can’t hear me” and that he takes his stuff. Y reported reluctance to go to the father’s home.
X was reported to say of the father “he used to be playful and gentle” and a “good dad”. He reported that he wanted “the dad I had before separation”, whom he described as funny and playful and kind. X observed that his brother finds it hard to say what he wants.
In those notes, Ms E also records that Y said that he was scared of the father but that he could not tell him that. He expressed that he was not like his brother, he did not want the father to get angry with him, but he wished he could be like his brother and stay with their mother.
Ms E gave direct feedback to the father about things discussed with the children. There is no doubt that the process would have been challenging for the father. However, Ms E notes that the father raised his voice, that he blamed the mother for X’s “loyalty bind” and that he became agitated.
The father wrote to Ms E the following day and shared his views about the purpose of the family therapy and his response to matters Ms E had shared with him. He described the mother’s actions in the court proceedings as wanting to take the children from him and that therapy was occurring in that environment. He asserted that “the entire engagement is false/fake. My ex wife is engaged in the process only for the benefit of the court case”. He ascribed to the mother, financial motivations for her actions concerning the children.
Ms E wrote to the father on 11 March 2024 expressing her alarm at his communication and his views of the process. Ms E shared her views of, and concerns for, each of the children in that letter and repeated her urging for counselling to be resumed for them.
There was no challenge made to the accuracy of Ms E’s notes, the contents of which were explored with her in her oral evidence. I accept her evidence as an accurate account of what had occurred in her sessions with the family.
The father claimed in his oral evidence that he did not know why Ms E stopped working with the family in March 2024. I do not accept that the father had any uncertainty about what had occurred. Ms E’s letter to him made clear that her observations of his engagement in the last session and the contents of his letter meant that she had resolved that she could not assist the family further.
The father opposed Ms E being reinstated to assist the family again, but by October 2024, the parties agreed to this occurring. Ms E was reinstated to undertake reportable family therapy. That therapy resumed, involving the parents and the children.
The evidence makes clear that Ms E experienced aspects of the father’s engagement in the therapy to be difficult. Based on her written documents (file notes and communications to the parties and others) and oral evidence, I am satisfied that Ms E at times experienced the father as angry and that he raised his voice and became dysregulated. Ms E described in her oral evidence, “reading the riot act” to the father and taking him out of session to enable him to recalibrate his engagement and behaviour.
Ms E again withdrew from assisting the family in November 2025. Ms E recorded in a note that she made on 1 November 2025 that she often felt that the father’s anger “spills into my room on me and after two months of this I’m not prepared to continue being in a room with him in this state as I can’t see any true change”.
Ms E’s engagement with the family into 2025 meant that she was able to provide direct evidence of more contemporary engagement with the children. I have been significantly assisted by her evidence, which was thoughtful and balanced. Of Y, Ms E indicated in her oral evidence that she had been more concerned for him than for X; that she observed him to describe events in one way and then in a starkly different way when his brother was present. She observed that Y would become distressed when challenged about the differing accounts he had given. She described Y as “disassociating” to manage the conflict he was experiencing in how to engage in his two different worlds, living across two households.
Ms E observed that Y’s sense of self was diminishing in his effort to manage the people around him. She opined that Y strives to manipulate his environment to minimise damage to himself, meaning that he is not able to hold his own views. Ms E opined that Y mirrors the views of those around him.
The challenges for Y managing all of this, she opined, were made harder given his other vulnerabilities. Ms E identified that these challenges for Y might be manifest in him acting out, becoming dysregulated, and finding it difficult when faced with parental opposition. In the longer term, Ms E observed that Y might be at risk of disengaging from his education and may experience mental health challenges, potentially including a depressive disorder.
Ms E agreed that as an adult, she was able to withdraw from engagement with the father when she experienced discomfort because of his conduct. She agreed that for a child, experiencing the dysregulation of a loved parent would cause them to be fearful. Ms E agreed in her oral evidence that when she observed the father to be angry and dysregulated, it was frightening, although she recognised that this was not the father’s intention.
Ms E agreed that it would be harder for Y, managing two different narratives depending on who was present, and that this was amplified by his ADHD.
Ms E agreed that the father’s dysregulation, when experienced by a child, would be emotionally abusive. She agreed that there was some risk that the father might experience a highly emotional state after the final hearing, which was more likely, if he experienced the outcome to be disappointing.
Ms E also spoke with Mr G, the psychologist engaged to assist the children after Ms D. She observed of those discussions that it was her impression that he had not thought about matters she had raised with him which were of concern for the children. While not critical of Mr G, Ms E considered that the children may have edited what they told him and that the therapy might “meander”.

The children’s schooling

In October 2023, the parties had agreed to enrol the children at a particular school and the necessary processes and paperwork had been completed. After the father became aware that the mother had advised the Child Support Agency of the changed living arrangements for X, the father advised the school that he withdrew his consent to the enrolment of the children.
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the father did so in retaliation for her actions and without regard for the impact upon the children. There is weight to that contention as the father did not at that time (or at the hearing) contend that he had otherwise concluded that the school would no longer meet the needs of the children. A court application was brought by the mother to secure the children’s attendance. Again, once at court, the father consented to orders for the children to attend the school, as previously agreed. I am satisfied that these events instance an occasion when the father has taken steps which suited his preferences (here, it would appear, to punish the mother for her engagement about child support) with little regard for the possible impact of such upon the children. His ultimate consent to orders confirming the agreed enrolment of the children reinforces that his actions were not focussed upon the benefits or otherwise of a particular school when he changed his mind about the enrolment.
In February 2025, Y experienced an injury at school following an altercation with a student. The father was dissatisfied with the school’s response to those events and determined that Y would be enrolled elsewhere.
There was a disagreement between the parents about the response to these events. The mother supported Y returning to school with the support, interventions and the management plan proposed by the school. The father did not agree and considered those responses insufficient to protect Y from further risk from the particular child involved.
There were orders in place at that time for Y to attend that school. While Y was in the mother’s care, he went to school (with shortened attendance as part of the return to school plan devised by the school) and while with the father, he did not go to school.
Y was in this fashion, experiencing directly the inability of his parents to reach a consensus, and I am satisfied that he would have been very aware of the conflict between his parents on this issue.
The father applied to the court to seek orders for Y to attend the school preferred by the father and the mother then agreed in orders made by consent on 4 March 2025, for Y to attend another school.
I accept that the father was genuine in his belief that Y was at further risk from the child at the school who had occasioned his injury and that he considered the school’s response to, and management of, these matters to have been inadequate. While the evidence makes clear that the father was determined that Y would not return to that school and his engagement around this issue was rigid, he did make an application to obtain orders to affect that outcome (and thus bring to an end his breach of the schooling order). The mother said that she felt, in the end, that she had little choice but to yield to the change of school as sought by the father.
There is no controversy between the parents that Y has transitioned well to the new school and appears settled in that environment.

The mother’s evidence

It was the mother’s evidence, not challenged in cross examination, that it had been Y’s longstanding desire to have less time with the father. The mother identifies, however, that Y is protective of the father and does not want to hurt him. The mother identifies that Y misses having his brother in the house with him when he is with the father.
In her affidavit, the mother sets out instances where Y has told her that he is afraid of the father.
The mother’s position before the court however, since 26 May 2025, has been that Y’s time with his father should be reduced to a weekend each fortnight during school terms and half of school holidays, among other things. The mother has been represented by lawyers throughout these proceedings. The relief sought by the mother in May 2025 and at the commencement of the final hearing, I accept, reflected the outcome which she considered at that time to be in the best interests of Y.
The mother received a telephone call from Y on the morning of the first day of the hearing (when Y was in the father’s care). A proof of evidence completed that morning by the mother formed part of her evidence in chief. The mother described that conversation with Y as out of character for him, that it was unusual and of concern to her, and that some of the language he used was not typical of the child.
I accept that Y’s phone call is suggestive of a child acutely caught in the conflict between his parents. The fact of the call being made suggests that Y was worried about the court event generally and what might be attributed to him as to his views in these proceedings. The call was made in the shadow of the child having, the week before, met with the ICL and the outline of case document of the ICL having been sent to the parties the night before.
I have no evidence that the father asked the child to make the call or directly influenced the things Y said. However, the content and timing of the call is suggestive of the worry Y is carrying about these proceedings and the impact of the things he has said to other people about his living arrangements and preferences. These actions are consistent with Ms E’s observations of Y’s tendency to subjugate his own comfort and preferences, to meet the preferences of others.
Notwithstanding the inclusion of this additional evidence in chief, at the commencement of her case, the mother continued to seek the relief as identified in May 2025.
The father indicated his intention not to cross examine the mother. Before the evidence commenced, I gave the father an opportunity to speak with the duty lawyer about that issue, and that occurred. The father subsequently confirmed his election not to cross examine the mother, having received legal advice.
Therefore, the mother’s primary evidence was not the subject of challenge through cross examination. The father made denials of certain matters (including about the mother’s allegations of family violence and controlling behaviours during the marriage) in his affidavit and I am satisfied that the mother’s counsel thoroughly put to the father the events and specific allegations made by the mother.
The mother describes the father’s conduct towards her as increasingly controlling during the relationship and that there were many occasions when she experienced his anger towards her and that she felt frightened (including, on occasion, that the father might hurt her and, or, the children, when he was driving at high speed).
I also have the evidence of the mother’s sister, Ms H, who directly observed aspects of the father’s engagement with the mother, instances of his controlling behaviour and his parenting of the children (which she described as very strict, overbearing and restrictive). Ms H assisted the parties in an attempt to reach agreement about the care arrangements for the children, and other things, in August 2023 (at which time Y was living in a shared care arrangement).
I also have regard to the evidence of the mother’s treating psychologist, Ms J. While Ms J was cross examined, that process did not lead her to change any part of her evidence.
I am satisfied that the mother experienced the father to be controlling of her and that she was fearful of him. I make that finding in circumstances where the mother’s evidence to that effect was not the subject of challenge and where I have the benefit of the evidence of Ms J, the psychologist upon whom the mother has attended and who has treated the mother for anxiety and trauma symptoms. In an assessment in April 2024, Ms J assessed the mother as being “in the moderate range for depression, anxiety and stress (92nd percentile)”. In an assessment in June 2024, Ms J assessed the mother as having clinically significant symptoms of PTSD (not meeting one criterion, being exposure to actual or threatened death).
Ms J noted that the mother reported clinically significant PTSD symptoms and that her symptoms had persisted for more than six months and “have caused her clinically significant distress”. In her opinion, which was not the subject of relevant challenge, Ms J considered the mother’s symptoms to be “directly related to (the mother’s) marriage and ongoing interpersonal difficulties with co-parenting with (the father)”. Ms J reported that the mother had “consistently acted respectfully towards (the father) ... she expressed a willingness for her children to have a relationship with (the father), whilst expressing her concerns for their safety and well-being”.
I also have the evidence of the mother’s partner, Ms K, as to her observations of Y including occasions when he has expressed reluctance about being with the father. Again, while the father cross examined Ms K, there was no impact upon or change to her evidence as a result of that process. Ms K relates of Y that he has said to her “he does not have the strength that [X] has to stand up to his dad, so he has to keep going to his dad’s.” Ms K said that Y has told her that, “he cannot speak freely to anyone if there is a chance his dad could find out what he has told them”. Ms K deposes to both children speaking to her of their “difficulties” in their relationship with the father and that “he does not shout at them quite as much as he used to but uses a deep menacing tone that scares and intimidates them”.
Ms K recounts an undated occasion when Y told her that his father said to him “I’m so angry with you I could punch you” and an occasion when the father pulled the child by his hair.
I accept the evidence of the mother and Ms K about their observations of Y. Neither was challenged about these accounts, and their observations are plausible and consistent with the observations of other people involved in Y’s care.
I accept the mother’s evidence (and as endorsed by Ms K in particular) that the mother has promoted the children’s relationship with the father and identifies benefits to the children in that relationship, while attempting to balance her concerns about risks to them in the father’s care.

THE SINGLE EXPERT REPORT

The Single Expert Report of Ms Buckley (“the Single Expert”) was released in August 2024 and the Single Expert had met with the parties and the children in June 2024. In circumstances where the Single Expert Report had not been updated, the Single Expert in her oral evidence, quite properly identified limits upon the extent to which she could comment upon more contemporary events. As identified in her report, the Single Expert had not spoken with the children’s medical providers, psychologists or the family therapist in circumstances where she had not been directed to do so in the terms of her engagement. The Single Expert also identified additional limitations to her written report, including that the parents were “poor historians and causing difficulties compiling a clear timeline” (page 3).
The Single Expert observed that following separation, the children had lived in a week about arrangement that continued “seemingly without incident” for some years and that the parents managed in that time “an amicable and collaborative parenting relationship” (paragraph 3). The Single Expert succinctly summarises the subsequent disputes between the parties about (paragraph 5):
... the value and purpose of the children’s counselling, how to manage [Y’s] ADHD, whether (the father’s) parenting practices are appropriate or excessively punitive, the fractured relationship between [X] and (the father) and (the father’s) suspicions that (the mother) is influencing the children to align with her, and whether the week about living arrangement needs to be changed.
The mother advised the Single Expert that she “was not scared” of the father and that “she was confident the children were safe in his care, especially when [Ms L] was present” (paragraph 25). The Single Expert states that the mother “did not identify any immediate safety or risk concerns for [Y] (with his father), she remained cautious, believing that he might still be at risk of experiencing harsh parenting practices in (the father’s) home” (paragraph 33).
The Single Expert notes of the father that he “impressed as a loving parent, speaking positively and knowledgeably about the children’s personalities, strengths, interests and achievements” (paragraph 50). The mother is also described as “a loving and protective parent” (paragraph 60). The mother is reported to have recognised of Y that he enjoys his time with his father but struggles being away from her (paragraph 61).
In the Single Expert Report, the Single Expert opined that the children had a warm and loving relationship with both of their parents. The report writer also identified that the parents’ relationship was marred by mutual mistrust and suspicion, which was negatively impacting the children’s experiences and relationships with each parent (paragraph 69).
The Single Expert observed the children with the father and said that neither child “showed signs of fear or discomfort” (paragraph 83) and that “both children have formed a close and connected relationship with their father” (paragraph 87).
The Single Expert had recommended that Y remain living in a shared arrangement across both homes, absent evidence to suggest he was unsafe or at risk in the father’s home (paragraph 121). In her discussions with Y, the Single Expert records that while he had expressed a desire to reduce his time with his father, he enjoyed living with him (and the wider family in the father’s home) but expressed a wish for his father to play more with him (paragraph 101). Later, the Single Expert notes that Y “expressed ambivalence about changing the current week about arrangements” (paragraph 104).
At paragraph 121, the Single Expert opines:
For [Y], the priority should be to ensure he receives consistent care, routine, management and appropriate consequences to effectively manage his ADHD in both homes. If this is not possible, a primary home may be warranted. Until then, in the absence of evidence to suggest that [Y] is unsafe in or at risk at (the father’s) home, the week about living arrangement should continue.
In her oral evidence, the Single Expert maintained that Y would benefit from continuing engagement with both of his parents and both sides of his family, provided that he was safe in those arrangements.

THE FATHER’S CASE

The father opposed any change for Y. He identified substantial risks to Y in the abrupt suspension of his longstanding care arrangements which had been in place, by agreement between the parents, for virtually the entire post separation period, and which the father said had served Y well.
When I invited submissions as to orders which may differ from those proposed by the parties, the father proffered alternative arrangements on an interim basis including time from Wednesday to Monday per fortnight and, otherwise, for defined orders in relation to various special occasions including about planned holidays.
The father relied upon the evidence before the court of the benefits Y derived when in the father’s care, including as observed by his wife, Ms L, and his brother, neither of whom were cross examined. Ms L appears to have adopted the narrative of the father with respect to criticism of the mother, and that lessens the weight I give to her evidence. The evidence of the father’s brother supports the father’s contention that Y enjoys his time with the father and their family. In the context of specific holiday events, Y is observed to be settled and to be having fun, and his needs are well met.
The father submitted that Y appears to be doing well at school, where his engagement is steady.
The father referred to the notes prepared by Mr G, the psychologist now providing care to Y. He pointed out that the most recent sessions do not suggest a child presenting with significant distress or challenge. I accept that in the February 2026 notes, Y reports to Mr G some positive experiences of his time during the holidays with the father. He also expresses that he was missing his brother during the holidays.
Review of those documents confirms that Y does not report to Mr G distress or concern relating to his living arrangements. Instead, he engages in discussions about challenges with peers at school. However, I am not persuaded that such necessarily means that Y is not experiencing those feelings relating to his care arrangements. Y had made clear to others, including to Ms K, that he was distrustful of sharing how he feels with psychologists, as the father finds out what is discussed.
The father has engaged in frequent text communication with Mr G and those texts were in evidence. They suggest that the father has brought Mr G into the litigation framework, sharing his views of the mother’s actions and motivations and providing “background” to Mr G about what is going on. The father asks Mr G to write a letter for him to use in court and states “they are attacking my capacity as a parent, so would be useful to have your insights to present...”. On the first day of the final hearing, the father texted Mr G expressing his thanks for his notes (produced to the court) and expresses “your notes on [Y] may just save the day”.
Even after the crisis events engulfing X in late 2025, the father criticised the mother’s actions to Mr G for acting unilaterally and against orders in securing other mental health support for X.
The father has placed Mr G at the centre of his litigation management and shares his strategy about the court process.
I identify, accordingly, that there is some risk that Y may not feel that he has effective therapeutic support and a safe environment in which to share how he is feeling and may be tailoring the things he shares with Mr G to ensure the process is safe for him.

DISCUSSION

The mother contends that the historical events, summarised above, where she and the father were in conflict about how best to respond to specific issues for the children, make clear that the father lacks insight about, and has acted to obstruct, the children’s essential supports. Each of the children have vulnerabilities in their health and developmental functioning, the management of which is made more acute in the context of the poor family dynamic. Criticism of the father’s approach to those important issues and his obstruction of the children’s receipt of psychological support can be fairly made, but those events more properly inform discussion around decision making in aid of preventing such discord in the future.
In addition, I accept the evidence of the mother, that the children have their own experiences of their father’s rigidity and anger and have observed their mother’s vulnerabilities in the face of her own experiences with the father. The mother has maintained her support of the children (and Y in particular) spending time with the father despite those experiences, as she identifies benefits to be derived by the children in spending time with their father.
Y has consistently reported that he would prefer less time with his father, and that he does not feel able to “stand up” to his father or behave as his brother has. I recognise that there was some ambivalence in Y’s statements to the Single Expert about this. I place greater weight upon the statements made by Y to those people with whom he had developed a therapeutic relationship of trust (formed in meetings over an extended period), which I find to be the case with respect to Ms E and Ms D. Y had repeat engagements with each of these psychologists, allowing them to build a foundation for trust and disclosure and for Y to be more comfortable in the discussions occurring with them.
Y is a vulnerable child, “who has been identified as neurodivergent, with a history emotional dysregulation, aggression, and academic and social challenges” (Single Expert Report paragraph 2). He needs to be shielded from the burdens of managing the conflict between his parents and decision making about his care arrangements.
Y’s efforts to manage how he presents to people around him, may result in his continuing loss of his sense of himself, as identified by Ms E, and impairs his psychological health.
I remain concerned for Y’s immediate well-being where, for an extended period of time, he has reported to adults engaged in his care and support that he is not managing the current living arrangements and the time in his father’s care. Those sentiments were repeated when Y recently met with the ICL, and Y’s phone call to his mother on the first day of the hearing is indicative of the pressure this child is experiencing.
Ms E identified some risk of psychological harm for Y in subverting his true preferences and responses to please those around him. The description by Ms E of Y’s “disassociation” means that this risk weighs heavily.
I also accept that there is a risk of psychological harm to Y if he were to experience his father to be angry or dysregulated (for example, as a product of this hearing). I am asked to assess that risk as being greater (indeed, as unacceptable) in circumstances where the father has shown limited insight into, and sensitivity about, the needs of the children on multiple occasions and where he has in some instances, obstructed appropriate supports for the children.
There is some weight to that argument. I accept that Y has experienced his father to be at times frightening and he has been exposed to his father’s angry and controlling behaviour, including to his mother during the marriage. The father has limited insight into the impacts of his behaviour on others. The mother has consistently identified these limitations in the father’s engagement with the children, but has, nonetheless, supported the children in spending substantial time in the father’s care.
There is no evidence before me of more recent or particular instances of the father behaving in a manner to the children in which the father has become angry or dysregulated, for example in the manner identified by Ms E of her experiences in November 2025. Even after the events at the last session of reportable family therapy, in which the mother participated, the mother had maintained that Y should still spend time with his father every second weekend in addition to time in the school holidays. The mother does not depose in her affidavit to events of concerning anger or frightening behaviour by the father, occurring since she amended her relief in her Amended Initiating Application in May 2025. Prior to May 2025, the mother had, since separation, supported Y living in a shared care arrangement with his father. Those matters inform the assessment of the likelihood of an event of dysregulation occurring in the future.
I am unable to identify in the evidence explored at the final hearing, instances of the father exhibiting anger and dysregulation about which the mother was not previously aware, to aid in explaining her decision to resile from her long-held position that it was in Y’s best interests to spend some time with the father. The mother had consistently maintained that there were some risks to the children in their father’s care, arising from aspects of his functioning, but she also identified sufficient benefits to that relationship that she sought orders promoting time.
At the conclusion of the hearing, the mother, in effect, contended that the risks to Y in his father’s care were now more apparent and, or, had increased such that they could no longer be mitigated and that it was in his best interests that his time with the father be immediately suspended, pending the final determination. On a final basis, the mother embraced the orders sought by the ICL, which provided that after a 6-month period where no time occurred, Y could spend time with the father in accordance with his wishes. That latter proposal sits at odds with the contention made on behalf of the mother at this time, that Y spending time with the father now places the child at an unacceptable risk of psychological harm.
The Full Court recently summarised the predictive exercise required to be undertaken by the court when assessing the risk of harm, in Novakoka & Novakoka [2026] FedCFamC1A at [24] as follows:
The assessment of risk is a predictive exercise based in the evidence, entailing the foresight of possible harm. Risks of harm are not susceptible of scientific demonstration or proof (CDJ v VAJ at [151]), but are instead postulated from known historical facts and present circumstances (Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 at [7]). Possibilities are a legitimate basis for finding that there is such a risk (Malec v JC Hutton Pty Ltd [1990] HCA 20; (1990) 169 CLR 638; Bant & Clayton [2015] FamCAFC 222; (2015) 53 Fam LR 621 at [99]), as long as there exists in the evidence a proper basis for those possibilities.
It was contended on behalf of the mother, that in the assessment of risk to Y of exposure to his father’s anger or dysregulation, that the risk was greater as a consequence of the difficult experience of this final hearing.
I am not persuaded at this interim stage, and pending my final determination, that there is an increased risk that the father may become dysregulated with Y, either because of the father’s experience of these proceedings or more generally. Indeed, the fact that the matter has not yet been resolved on a final basis may act as a protective measure, given that the father will have the benefit of these reasons and will be conscious that an adverse experience for Y may lead to an application being made to re-open the evidence.
To the extent the mother contends that, at the conclusion of the evidence, she now identifies the harm which Y is at risk of experiencing (in the event of his father becoming dysregulated) as being more serious and ongoing, I am not able, without the opportunity to carefully review all of the evidence and the benefit of transcript, to identify the basis for that changed assessment. I also note as identified previously, the inconsistency between the outcomes sought by the mother on an interim basis and the final orders sought which in effect place responsibility for risk assessment upon the child.
The mother’s counsel submitted that the court would see little efficacy in making orders to regulate or place limits upon the father’s conduct in circumstances where he has shown himself prepared to ignore court orders. While I am satisfied that there have been occasions when the father has acted contrary to obligations imposed by orders, by and large, the parties have complied with matters agreed between them and set out in orders. Where the final proceedings are not formally concluded and the matter remains reserved, poor conduct by a parent may result in a party making a further application to the court.
I also identify that if I made the orders sought by the mother on an interim basis, and if I was to later order the resumption of time between Y and his father as part of my final determination, the child would have been exposed to the upheaval of substantial change, contrary to his best interests.
I also place some weight on the risk that despite the efforts of adults caring for him, Y may become anxious about his father’s welfare if all time was to be immediately suspended. While I acknowledge that the child is not responsible for the father’s well-being, the evidence makes clear that this child is already susceptible to internalising responsibility for others.

DETERMINATION

The approach now urged by the mother and the ICL implicitly contends that there is greater risk to the child in spending time with the father, with limited identification of the benefits to Y in having a relationship with his father (and wider paternal family). The mother and ICL contend that, in keeping with the provisions of the Act, the court should not make orders (or continue orders already made) unless it is satisfied that it is safe to do so.
I will not accede to the application made on behalf of the mother. I am satisfied that, subject to additional orders I propose to make, the risk to Y of experiencing his father’s anger and, or, dysregulation may be appropriately mitigated. I recognise that the risks identified previously for Y warrant some changes being made to his circumstances at this time, pending the final determination. The changes I will make will go some way to addressing the matters identified previously, in Y’s best interests.
As I identified to the parties during submissions, the court is not bound by the orders sought by the parties and the ICL ((U v U [2002] HCA 36; (2002) 211 CLR 238 at [284]–[285] and [263]), as referred to in Oberlin & Infeld [2021] FamCAFC 66 at [22]).
While I undertake a further review of the evidence (including with receipt of transcript) pending delivery of final reasons, I am minded to reduce the time Y spends with his father. I have determined in this exercise of balancing the considerations identified previously, that Y’s time with his father during school terms should be reduced to Friday to Monday every second weekend. This will ensure that Y’s relationship with his father and extended family will continue pending my final determination, while also allowing Y to experience some break from the current arrangement. I am persuaded on the evidence that Y has experienced aspects of his current living arrangements as challenging for some time now. Y will benefit from having more time with his mother to provide a balancing of his experiences with his father but freed from the burden of having to decide those changes himself and, or, managing the psychological pressure of navigating his perceptions of his parents’ preferences.
I also make clear in the orders and in these reasons, my expectation that there will be no engagement by the parents in discussions with Y or X about what has occurred or what was said in the proceedings. That order will also assist to mitigate the risk of Y experiencing “fallout” from his father about these changes which I make to his living arrangements.
I am not currently satisfied that it is likely or probable that if Y continues to spend some limited time with the father that the child will necessarily experience the father to behave in a dysregulated or angry way. I do not have specific evidence that such an event in fact has recently occurred, save for Ms E’s observations and engagement with the family in November 2025. Despite providing feedback to the parents about those events and discontinuing her engagement, the mother maintained the relief as sought in her May 2025 Amended Initiating Application, which I am entitled to accept as her support for time between Y and his father continuing.
The more limited time that Y will have with the father by necessity means that there is less time in which something might occur which causes Y difficulty, while still allowing Y to experience the benefits of spending time with the father, including engaging in holiday activities which the evidence makes clear he has previously enjoyed. In the balancing exercise, the making of the interim orders sought by the mother would be to deny the child the opportunity to experience the benefits which I am satisfied he derives from time with his father.
While the father suggested a longer block of time each fortnight, I see benefit for Y during the reserved period, in having shorter, defined periods with his father and longer time with his mother and brother in their home.
I am satisfied that this outcome is in the best interests of Y, having due regard to the risks identified previously, while allowing Y to experience the benefits of continuing his connection and relationship with his father and his extended family.
The parties will be able to facilitate Y’s changeover via school, minimising opportunities for exposure and potential conflict between them and the adverse impact of such for Y.
I recognise that school holiday time is an opportunity for children to engage in different activities with parents and without the pressures of fixed time commitments and the school transitions of term time. There is evidence of Y engaging comfortably with the father and the father’s household during holiday periods, particularly so when he has the benefit of the involvement of others, acting in effect as protective supports for Y. I recognise that Y misses his brother’s engagement with him on these occasions. I do not propose, at this interim stage, to revisit the school holiday and Easter arrangements agreed by the parties in October 2024 as each party will have made plans about the impending holidays in the lead up to the final hearing, the disruption of which may also be difficult for the child.
As a consequence of the orders I made on 13 March 2026, suspending the week about arrangements for Y pending the completion of submissions, Y has remained in the mother’s care. On my understanding of the commencement of school holidays, and in the interests of ensuring that the previous pattern of weekends with each parent is maintained, the first weekend Y will be in his father’s care is 1 May 2026. However, before that date, Y will have school holiday time in each household at the end of this school term.
I will again direct the ICL to discuss these orders with the children such that the message is clear that this outcome is the product of a decision by the court. I will also continue the order which makes express the obligation upon the parents not to speak with the children about the evidence or the proceedings. The matters addressed above make clear the reasons for that order. While each parent may face the children, particularly Y, seeking reassurance about these orders, it is important for the children that there is no exposition of what each parent said or did in the proceedings and that they are informed that these matters were determined by a judge. The orders I make will allow such to occur.
The mother’s evidence and that of her partner and the mother’s treating psychologist, makes clear that the mother finds communication with the father to be difficult and at times, distressing. I accept that the nature of the father’s communication to the mother has at times been very poor and on occasions, offensive.
In the interests of supporting the psychological safety of the mother (with related benefits to the children), I will continue the order I made on 13 March 2026 which requires communication from the father to be directed to the mother’s solicitors (save for any urgent matters in relation to the children).
As identified previously, there may be some risk to Y in managing ongoing direct communications with the father, especially in the shadow of the changes I have ordered. It is protective of the children, pending my final determination, that other communication between the father and the children be as agreed or at the mother’s instigation. I accept, and I find, that the mother has the necessary insight and established parenting strengths to support the children’s relationship with their father in this fashion. Until the conclusion of the evidence, the mother had shown her continuing ability to support the children’s relationship with the father, despite her own vulnerabilities deriving from his conduct in the relationship and since separation. That capacity by the mother leads me to accept that the mother will manage these arrangements in a child focussed manner.
Similarly, in her interim relief, the mother sought certain injunctions about the father’s attendance at her home and place of work. I accept the unchallenged evidence of the mother that she did not want the father to have her home address but that he holds this information. Under these orders, there is no need for the father to attend the mother’s home. However, I will continue the injunction made on the last occasion to provide additional reassurance to the mother, given her vulnerabilities relating to her experiences of family violence by the father.
The restraints I have made also exclude the father from attendance at the children’s school during periods that they are in the mother’s care, unless agreed in writing. Again, I am confident that the parties will capably manage those occasions, if any, during the reserved period where the children may benefit from the father’s attendance.
